subject: Internal medicine
title: Hyponatremia
preambleMarkdown:
# Hyponatremia

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Hyponatremia is a clinical condition or syndrome in internal medicine.
- This article focuses on adult presentation, differential diagnosis, diagnostics, complications, chronic management, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Hyponatremia to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Hyponatremia is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Hyponatremia depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In internal medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Hyponatremia.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Hyponatremia disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Hyponatremia should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Hyponatremia first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Hyponatremia, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Hyponatremia, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused internal medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Hyponatremia presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Hyponatremia with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Classify by tonicity, symptom severity, duration, volume status, urine osmolality, and urine sodium.
- First decide whether Hyponatremia is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Hyponatremia should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Seizure, coma, or severe symptoms require hypertonic saline with careful correction limits.
- Avoid overcorrection because osmotic demyelination risk rises in chronic hyponatremia.
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Hyponatremia is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Hyponatremia, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Hyponatremia may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Hyponatremia patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Hyponatremia, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Hyponatremia in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Hyponatremia, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Hyponatremia does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Hyponatremia may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Hyponatremia, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Hyponatremia as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treat symptoms first, then classify.
- Chronic hyponatremia must be corrected slowly.
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Hyponatremia draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Hyponatremia

_Source lines: 27302-27496._

```text
Hyponatremia
A. General Characteristics
1. This refers to too much water in relation to sodium in the serum.

2. It is typically defined as a plasma Na + concentration <135 mEq/L.

3. Symptoms usually begin when the Na + level falls to <120 mEq/L.

An important exception is increased intracranial pressure (ICP)
(e.g., after head injury). As ECF osmolality decreases, water shifts
into brain cells, further increasing ICP. (Therefore, it is critical to
keep serum sodium normal or slightly high in such patients.)

Quick HIT
- Hyponatremia and hypernatremia are caused by too much or
too little water.

- Hypovolemia and hypervolemia are caused by too little or too
much sodium.

B. Causes and Classification (based on serum osmolality)
1. Hypotonic hyponatremia-"true hyponatremia"-serum
osmolality <280 mOsm/kg (can be measured or calculated using
sodium, BUN, and glucose)
a. Hypovolemic
- Low urine sodium (<10 mEq/L)-implies increased sodium
retention by the kidneys to compensate for extrarenal
losses (e.g., diarrhea, vomiting, nasogastric suction,

diaphoresis, third-spacing, burns, pancreatitis) of sodium-
containing fluid.

- High urine sodium (>20 mEq/L)-renal salt loss is likely
due to tubular dysfunction-for example, diuretic excess,
decreased aldosterone (i.e., RTA type IV, causes include ACE
inhibitors and ARBs), ATN.

d. Euvolemic-no evidence of ECF expansion or contraction on
clinical grounds
- SIADH: Causes are numerous and include CNS pathologies,
malignancy (most commonly small cell lung cancer), surgery
(postoperative hyponatremia), drugs (e.g., SSRIs, oxytocin,
carbamazepine, haloperidol, cyclophosphamide, certain
antineoplastic agents), infections (HIV, pulmonary
infections), and more
- Hypothyroidism: mechanism not fully understood
- Adrenal insuficiency: cause is multifactorial
- Psychogenic polydipsia (urine osmolality <100)
- Administration/intake of a relative excess of free water-if a
patient is given D5W (or other hypotonic solution) to
replace fluids, or if water alone is consumed after intensive
exertion (with profuse sweating)
- Low dietary solute intake: kidneys excrete free water by
diluting urine, however all urine must contain some solute;
therefore individuals with poor diets (beer drinkers, elderly
persons with "tea and toast" diet) with a relative deficiency
of these solutes will be limited in daily free water excretion.

This will also have a low urine osmolality <100
k. Hypervolemic (low urine sodium)-This is due to water-
retaining states. The relative excess of water in relation to
sodium results in hyponatremia.

- CHF
- Nephrotic syndrome (renal failure)
- Liver disease
Quick HIT
Excessive water intake alone rarely leads to hyponatremia because
the kidneys have a great capacity to excrete water.

1. Isotonic hyponatremia (pseudohyponatremia)
a. An increase in plasma solids (e.g., proteins or lipids) artificially
lowers the plasma sodium concentration measurement via
laboratory artifact, but the amount of sodium in plasma is

normal (hence, pseudohyponatremia).

b. This can be caused by any condition that leads to elevated
protein or lipid levels.

2. Hypertonic hyponatremia
a. Caused by the presence of osmotic substances that cause an
osmotic shift of water out of cells. These substances cannot
cross the cell membrane and therefore create osmotic gradients
b. These substances include:
- Glucose-Hyperglycemia increases osmotic pressure, and
water shifts from cells into ECF leading to a dilutional
hyponatremia. For every 100 mg/dL increase in blood
glucose level above normal, the serum sodium level
decreases about 3 mEq/L. Note that the actual sodium
content in the ECF is unchanged.

- Mannitol, sorbitol, glycerol, maltose
- Radiocontrast agents
Quick HIT
As opposed to acute water intoxication, when hyponatremia
develops gradually (over a few days), the clinical features do not
appear until a relatively lower sodium level is reached.

C. Clinical Features
1. Neurologic symptoms predominate-caused by "water
intoxication"-osmotic water shifts, which leads to increased ICF
volume, specifically brain cell swelling or cerebral edema
a. Headache, delirium, irritability
b. Muscle twitching, weakness
c. Hyperactive deep tendon reflexes
2. Increased ICP, seizures, coma
3. GI-nausea, vomiting, ileus, watery diarrhea
4. Cardiovascular-hypertension due to increased ICP
5. Increased salivation and lacrimation
6. Oliguria progressing to anuria-may not be reversible if therapy
is delayed and acute renal failure develops
D. Diagnosis
1. Plasma osmolality-low in a patient with true hyponatremia
(Figure 8-2)
2. Urine osmolality
a. Low if the kidneys are responding appropriately by diluting the
urine-for example, primary polydipsia

b. Elevated if there are increased levels of ADH-for example,
SIADH, CHF, and hypothyroidism
3. Urine sodium concentration
a. Urine Na + should be low in the setting of hyponatremia
b. Urine Na + concentration >40 mmol/L is consistent with
SIADH, salt-wasting nephropathy or hypoaldosteronism.

Diuretics may produce this as well
c. Urine Na + concentration <20 mmol/L is consistent with
hypovolemia
A 59-year-old woman presents with fatigue and weakness and is found to
have a serum sodium of 126 mEq/L. Her medical history includes GERD
and anxiety, and her medications include aspirin, pantoprazole, and
sertraline. Her examination is unremarkable. Her urine studies reveal a
urine sodium of 74 mEq/L and a urine osmolality of 420 mOsm/kg.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A. Psychogenic polydipsia
B. Heart failure
C. SIADH
D. Hypovolemia
E. Adrenal insuficiency
- The answer is C: SIADH. The patient presents with moderate
hyponatremia, and her urine osmolality is >100 which essentially
rules out (A) psychogenic polydipsia as well as low solute intake (tea
and toast or beer potomania). Her urine sodium is >40, and in the
absence of diuretic use this is not suggestive of diagnoses of (D)
hypovolemia or (B) heart failure, which would have a low urine
sodium <20. Thus, these findings are suggestive of SIADH, which
may be caused by her SSRI sertraline. (E) Adrenal insuficiency is
another cause of euvolemic hyponatremia, but there is nothing to
suggest this diagnosis in the vignette.

FIGURE
8-2 Evaluation of hyponatremia.

Quick HIT
Most cases of hypernatremia are due to the following types of free
water loss:
- Nonrenal loss: insensible loss, GI tract (diarrhea)
- Renal loss: osmotic diuresis, diabetes insipidus
E. Treatment
1. Isotonic and hypertonic hyponatremias-diagnose and treat the
underlying disorder
2. Hypotonic hyponatremia
a. Depends on the underlying condition, which should always be
addressed, as well as severity (mild Na 130-134, moderate 120-

129, and severe <120) and the chronicity (acute hyponatremia
can be corrected more quickly)
b. The majority of patients should have free water restriction
c. Volume status will determine diuresis versus giving IVF
d. Severe (Na + <120 mmol/L) or if symptomatic-give
hypertonic saline to increase serum sodium by 1 to 2 mEq/L/hr
until symptoms improve
- Hypertonic saline rapidly increases the tonicity of ECF.

- Do not increase sodium more than 8 mmol/L during the first
24 hours. An overly rapid increase in serum sodium
concentration may produce central pontine demyelination
- Rate of correction: Maximum 8 mEq/L in 24 hours to
prevent overcorrection and osmotic demyelination
syndrome, however in symptomatic patients aim for
correcting 4 to 6 mEq/L in first 6 hours. Make sure to check
sodium levels frequently
```

### Source Excerpt: Sodium Imbalance

_Source lines: 14895-15087._

```text
I. SODIUM IMBALANCE
A. Hyponatremia

## 1. Discussion

a. Definition. Serum sodium level ! 135 mEq/L
i. True hyponatremia. Serum sodium concentration ! 125 mEq/L and a
serum osmolality ! 250 mOsm/kg
ii. Pseudohyponatremia. Secondary to severe hyperglycemia, hyperprotein-
emia, or hyperlipidemia. Serum sodium level is decreased but the total
body sodium level is unchanged.

b. Causes
i. Hypovolemic hyponatremia
a) Extrarenal losses. Hypovolemic hyponatremia as a result of extrare-
nal sodium loss, urinary sodium level ! 20 mEq/L.

b) Renal losses. Hypovolemic hyponatremia as a result of renal sodium
loss, the urinary sodium level "20 mEq/L.

ii. Euvolemic hyponatremia. Occurs due to an increase in total body
water; the total body sodium level is normal. Hypothyroidism and
the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone are causes of
euvolemic hyponatremia.

iii. Hypervolemic hyponatremia. Occurs with renal failure, cirrhosis, con-
gestive heart failure, and nephrotic syndrome
2. Clinical features. The influx of water into brain cells may lead to apathy, agi-
tation, headaches, altered consciousness, seizures, coma, weakness, nausea,
anorexia, and vomiting.

3. Differential diagnoses. Includes pseudohyponatremia, which can be caused
by diabetic ketoacidosis and a hyperosmolar state (leading to hyperglycemia),
multiple myeloma (leading to hyperproteinemia), or hypertriglyceridemia
(leading to hyperlipidemia)

## 4. Evaluation

a. Laboratory studies. Include a serum electrolyte panel (i.e., sodium,
potassium, chloride, and bicarbonate); serum glucose, blood urea nitrogen
[BUN], and creatinine levels; and urinalysis (to determine the urine sodium
level and osmolality)
b. Electrocardiography. An electrocardiogram (ECG) should be obtained to
evaluate for arrhythmia.

## 5. Therapy

a. Fluid restriction or replacement
i. Hypervolemic and euvolemic hyponatremia usually result from hemo-
dilution; fluid restriction is the initial treatment in stable, asymptom-
atic patients. Inhibition of water reabsorption caused by syndrome of
inappropriate antidiuretic hormone can be treated on an inpatient basis
using demeclocycline (600 to 1,200 mg/day) or furosemide.

ii. Hypovolemic hyponatremia can usually be treated with isotonic saline.

Quick 	HIT
Sweating, vomiting,
diarrhea, and third-space
sequestration (e.g., as a
result of burns, peritonitis,
or pancreatitis) are common
sources of extrarenal sodium
loss.

Quick 	HIT
Loop or osmotic diuretics,
Addison disease, ketonuria,
and renal tubular acidosis are
sources of renal sodium loss.

Quick 	HIT
Syndrome of inappropriate
antidiuretic hormone is
associated with tumors,
central nervous system (CNS)
disease, pulmonary disease,
hypopituitarism, medications,
idiopathic causes, and a reset
osmostat.

Diseases of the Cardiovascular System
Metabolic Emergencies
b. Sodium replacement. Sodium deficits can be calculated as follows:
Na# deficit (mEq) $ 0.6 % (weight kg) % (140 & serum Na# )
i. If the hyponatremia is acute, is severe (i.e., the serum sodium level is
less than 120 mEq/L), and results in CNS symptoms, administration of
3% (hypertonic) saline solution at 25 to 60 mL/hour is indicated.

a) The serum sodium concentration should not increase at a rate that
exceeds 2 mEq/hour.

b) Hypertonic saline should be discontinued when sodium levels in-
crease to 120 mEq/L or when the patient shows significant clinical
improvement.

ii. In patients with chronic severe hyponatremia, the rate of serum sodium
correction should not exceed 0.5 mEq/L/hour (12 mEq/L/day). If the
hyponatremia is severe (i.e., serum sodium levels less than 120 mEq/L)
and develops rapidly with CNS manifestations, 3% saline should be
administered.

## 6. Disposition

a. Admission . Symptomatic patients, serum sodium concentration below
125 mEq/L (with or without symptoms), patients who require intravenous
or pharmacologic correction of the sodium imbalance, and patients who
have significant comorbid factors (e.g., diabetes, advanced age, sepsis)
b. Discharge. If the patient is being discharged, the case should be discussed
with a primary care physician and a follow-up appointment should take
place within 48 to 72 hours.

B. Hypernatremia

## 1. Discussion

a. Definition. Serum sodium levels greater than 155 mEq/L
b. Causes
i. Reduced water intake can be caused by a defective thirst mechanism,
unconsciousness, an inability to drink, or a lack of access to water.

ii. Increased water loss can be caused by vomiting, diarrhea, sweating,
fever, hyperventilation, diabetes insipidus, osmotic diuresis, thyrotoxi-
cosis, and severe burns.

iii. Increased sodium intake or renal salt retention is due to hypertonic
saline ingestion or infusion, sodium bicarbonate administration, hyper-
aldosteronism, Cushing disease, and congenital adrenal hyperplasia.

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms. Confusion, weakness, muscle irritability, tremulousness, sei-
zures, and coma
b. Physical examination findings. Flat neck veins, orthostatic hypotension,
tachycardia, poor skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, tonic spasms, and
respiratory paralysis

## 3. Evaluation

a. Laboratory studies should include a complete blood count (CBC); serum
electrolyte panel; serum glucose, BUN, and creatinine levels; urinalysis,
urine sodium level, and osmolality.

b. Electrocardiography. An ECG should be obtained.

## 4. Therapy

a. Fluid replacement. The amount of water needed to correct hypernatremia
can be estimated as follows:
Water deficit (L) $ 0.6 % (usual body weight in kg) % (measured Na#
concentration & desired Na# concentration) / measured Na# concentration
i. When dehydration is severe, normal saline or lactated Ringer solution
should be administered to improve blood pressure and tissue perfusion.

ii. Once perfusion is reestablished, 0.45% saline is administered to main-
tain a urine output of 0.5 mL/kg/hour.

b. Sodium reduction. The rate of sodium reduction should not exceed 10 to
15 mEq/L/day. The goal is to reach a normal serum sodium value in 48 to
72 hours.

Quick 	HIT
Correcting the sodium
deficiency too quickly could
cause central pontine
myelinolysis.

Quick 	HIT
Hypocalcemia, which is
frequently seen in patients
with hypernatremia, may
contribute to the neurologic
symptoms.

M E T A B O L I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	185

Diseases of the Cardiovascular System
Metabolic Emergencies

## 5. Disposition

a. Admission. Symptomatic patients, serum sodium concentration greater
than 160 mEq/L (with or without symptoms), patients who require intra-
venous or pharmacologic correction of the sodium imbalance, and patients
who have significant comorbid factors require admission.

b. Discharge. Before discharging a patient, the case should be discussed with
a primary care physician and arrangements should be made for appropriate
follow-up.
```

<!-- source: step-up-emergency-medicine import end -->
